description: Longevity-associated variants — genes and pathways replicated in human longevity cohort studies

CC.interpretation: Common KLOTHO genotype — standard FGF23 signaling, bone mineral metabolism, and aging trajectory. No KL-VS-associated longevity effect.
recommendations: Optimize lifestyle factors to maintain cognitive and kidney health

CT.interpretation: Single KL-VS copy — associated with ~30% reduced Alzheimer's risk (Dubal 2014, Nature), ~7% higher frontal lobe volume, improved FGF23-mediated phosphate regulation, and longevity advantage in multiple cohorts (Hum Genet 2005). The heterozygous advantage: one copy outperforms both no copies and two copies.
recommendations: Prioritize cognitive stimulation to leverage this genetic advantage
Vitamin D adequacy supports FGF23 pathway that KLOTHO regulates
Aerobic exercise amplifies KLOTHO expression further
Low-phosphate diet (avoid processed food phosphate additives) supports KL-WT pathway

TT.interpretation: Two copies of KL-VS — rare genotype. Some studies show heterozygous benefit is not doubled; at advanced age, homozygous KL-VS may not confer additional longevity advantage over CT, possibly due to signaling saturation.
recommendations: Same lifestyle recommendations as CT carriers
Cognitive stimulation and aerobic exercise remain key
TT.scienceSimplified: KLOTHO is your anti-aging hormone — one copy of the KL-VS variant is associated with better brain health and longevity. Having one copy (not two) appears to be the sweet spot.

TT.effect: Homozygous longevity allele — strongest FOXO3 survival benefit
TT.interpretation: Strongest replicated longevity genotype — associated with exceptional lifespan (85+), lower cardiovascular mortality, and healthier aging across Japanese, German, Italian, and US cohorts
recommendations: Intermittent fasting / time-restricted eating — the key FOXO3 pathway activator
Regular aerobic exercise — FOXO3 mediates anti-aging exercise effects
Low-glycemic diet to maintain low insulin/IGF-1
Resveratrol 250-500mg (activates SIRT1, which feeds into FOXO3)
Metformin or berberine discussion with physician for further longevity pathway support

GT.effect: One longevity allele — moderate FOXO3 benefit
GT.interpretation: One copy of the replicated longevity allele — partial FOXO3 activity enhancement and moderate longevity signal
recommendations: Intermittent fasting and aerobic exercise maximize FOXO3 pathway
Low-glycemic diet to maintain favorable insulin/IGF-1 signaling

GG.interpretation: Wild-type FOXO3 — longevity depends more heavily on lifestyle inputs than for carriers of the T allele
recommendations: Caloric restriction or fasting regimens especially impactful without the genetic head-start
Regular exercise is the most potent FOXO3 activator available to GG individuals
Berberine 500mg twice daily — activates AMPK/FOXO3 pathway

AA.effect: Val/Val — lowest CETP activity, highest HDL, longevity-associated
AA.interpretation: Reduced cholesteryl ester transfer protein — significantly higher HDL particle count and size; overrepresented in Ashkenazi Jewish centenarians (Barzilai 2003, JAMA); associated with lower coronary artery disease incidence
recommendations: Maintain this favorable lipid phenotype with Mediterranean diet
Regular aerobic exercise (further raises HDL)
Avoid trans fats and refined carbohydrates
HDL > 60 mg/dL is a realistic target with this genotype

AG.interpretation: Intermediate CETP activity — moderately elevated HDL and partial longevity signal
recommendations: Mediterranean diet and exercise to maximize HDL benefit

GG.interpretation: Standard CETP activity — no inherited HDL longevity advantage; HDL levels depend more heavily on lifestyle
recommendations: Aerobic exercise and Mediterranean diet especially important for HDL optimization without this genetic advantage
Consider niacin discussion with physician if HDL persistently below 40 mg/dL

GG.interpretation: Arg72 p53 isoform — more efficiently induces apoptosis in damaged cells (5-10x more potent mitochondrial pathway); stronger early cancer suppression but may contribute to greater age-related cell loss in some tissues
recommendations: Strong cancer prevention lifestyle complements this pro-apoptotic genotype
Avoid carcinogens that create DNA damage (smoke, UV, processed meats)
Adequate antioxidants (vitamin C, E, CoQ10) to minimize oxidative DNA damage load

GC.interpretation: Both Pro72 and Arg72 isoforms active — intermediate balance between transcriptional and mitochondrial apoptosis pathways
recommendations: Standard cancer prevention practices

CC.interpretation: Pro72 p53 isoform — favors cell cycle arrest over apoptosis; may allow more damaged cells to repair rather than being eliminated; associated with longevity in some European elderly cohorts
recommendations: DNA repair support: sulforaphane (NRF2 activator), zinc, NAD+ precursors (NMN/NR)
Regular cancer screening per age/sex guidelines

GG.interpretation: Attenuated insulin/IGF-1 pathway — associated with longevity (caloric restriction mimicry), lower cancer and age-related disease risk in EPIC cohort analyses
recommendations: Low-glycemic diet to maintain low IGF-1 (complements genetic tendency)
Limit dairy (primary dietary driver of IGF-1 elevation)
Intermittent fasting — reduces circulating IGF-1 further
Resistance training to maintain lean mass (local IGF-1 signaling in muscle remains beneficial)

AG.interpretation: One longevity allele — partial attenuation of IGF-1 receptor activity
recommendations: Low-glycemic and intermittent fasting beneficial to complement genetic tendency

AA.interpretation: Standard IGF-1 receptor activity — no inherited longevity pathway attenuation
recommendations: Low-glycemic diet and periodic fasting especially impactful (no genetic head start on IGF-1 pathway)
Consider monitoring serum IGF-1 annually if concerned about cancer risk

AA.interpretation: AMPK regulatory subunit variant — AMPK is the master energy sensor linking caloric restriction to longevity pathways (SIRT1, FOXO3, mTOR inhibition); this variant modulates cellular energy sensing
recommendations: AMPK activators are especially relevant: berberine 500mg twice daily, EGCG (green tea)
Exercise maximally activates AMPK regardless of genotype
Metformin discussion with physician (potent AMPK activator)
Caloric restriction or 16:8 intermittent fasting — triggers sustained AMPK activation

AG.interpretation: Heterozygous AMPK beta-1 variant — intermediate energy sensing phenotype
recommendations: Standard AMPK-supportive lifestyle: exercise, fasting, limited calorie excess

GG.interpretation: Standard AMPK energy sensing — normal longevity pathway activation response to caloric restriction and exercise
recommendations: Exercise and intermittent fasting remain the primary AMPK activators

GG.interpretation: Secondary FOXO3 longevity-associated allele — independent longevity signal at a different position in the FOXO3 locus; replicated in the UK Biobank
recommendations: Complements rs2802292 signal; same FOXO3-activating lifestyle strategies apply
Intermittent fasting and aerobic exercise

AG.effect: One longevity allele at secondary FOXO3 locus
recommendations: FOXO3 pathway activation through fasting and exercise

AA.interpretation: Wild-type at this secondary FOXO3 position
recommendations: Behavioral FOXO3 activation remains the primary lever

CC.interpretation: Ala300 ATG16L1 variant — reduced autophagy flux and increased inflammatory signaling; associated with Crohn's disease and impaired cellular housekeeping; autophagy is a key longevity pathway
recommendations: Fasting (16:8 or longer) is the strongest autophagy activator — especially impactful for CC individuals
Spermidine 1-2mg daily (polyamine that activates autophagy independent of mTOR)
Rapamycin discussion with physician (mTOR inhibitor, potent autophagy inducer)
Omega-3 reduces inflammatory burden compensating for reduced ATG16L1 activity

TC.interpretation: One reduced-activity allele — partial autophagy impairment
recommendations: Regular fasting intervals and spermidine-containing foods (wheat germ, aged cheese, soy)

TT.interpretation: Wild-type autophagy — efficient cellular housekeeping and protein aggregate clearance
recommendations: Fasting and caloric restriction still activate autophagy but less urgently needed

TT.effect: Risk allele homozygous — elevated T2D and longevity impact
TT.interpretation: KCNQ1 T allele — the most replicated type 2 diabetes GWAS locus in East Asian populations; associated with beta-cell function impairment and reduced insulin secretion; T2D is a major accelerator of biological aging
recommendations: Low-glycemic diet strictly (carbohydrate quality paramount for KCNQ1 risk genotype)
Resistance training 3x/week (increases beta-cell function independent of KCNQ1)
Regular HbA1c monitoring (quarterly if impaired fasting glucose)
Berberine 500mg twice daily — improves beta-cell function

CT.effect: One risk allele — moderate T2D risk elevation
CT.interpretation: Heterozygous KCNQ1 — intermediate beta-cell function impairment tendency
recommendations: Low-glycemic diet and annual HbA1c monitoring

CC.interpretation: Standard KCNQ1-mediated potassium channel function — standard insulin secretion
recommendations: Standard metabolic health practices

TT.effect: Arg262/Arg262 — pro-longevity but elevated autoimmune risk
TT.interpretation: SH2B3/LNK Arg262 variant — pleiotropic effects; positive selection driven by protection against infection in historical context; associated with longer lifespan in large cohort studies but also modestly elevated autoimmune risk
recommendations: Anti-inflammatory diet to balance the autoimmune tendency
Regular CRP and inflammatory marker monitoring
The longevity benefit appears to outweigh autoimmune risk in most environmental contexts
Standard immune health and anti-inflammatory practices

CC.interpretation: Wild-type Trp262 — no SH2B3-associated longevity effect from this variant
recommendations: Lifestyle factors are the primary lever for healthspan extension

AA.interpretation: Third reported FOXO3 locus longevity association (independent of rs2802292 and rs2802288); each FOXO3 longevity allele adds to cumulative FOXO3 pathway activation
recommendations: FOXO3 pathway activation via fasting, exercise, and low IGF-1 diet
Carriers of multiple FOXO3 longevity variants have compounding benefit — maintain the lifestyle

AG.effect: One allele at tertiary FOXO3 locus
recommendations: Same FOXO3 activation lifestyle strategies

GG.interpretation: Wild-type at this tertiary FOXO3 position
recommendations: Behavioral FOXO3 activation remains primary

rs7153922.name: P477T
rs7153922.display: GHR Growth Hormone Receptor / IGF-1 Axis

TT.effect: Reduced GHR signaling — attenuated IGF-1 production
TT.interpretation: Growth hormone receptor variant reduces GH-stimulated IGF-1 production; lower IGF-1 levels associated with longevity and reduced cancer risk (mTOR/PI3K pathway less activated)
recommendations: Low-glycemic and lower-protein diet to complement reduced IGF-1 tendency
Annual serum IGF-1 monitoring — target 120-200 ng/mL for balance of longevity and muscle maintenance
Resistance training to maintain lean mass without relying on IGF-1 elevation

TC.interpretation: Heterozygous — moderate GH receptor variant effect
recommendations: Standard growth hormone and IGF-1 health practices

CC.effect: Normal GHR signaling — standard IGF-1 production
CC.interpretation: Wild-type GHR — normal growth hormone receptor sensitivity
recommendations: Caloric restriction and fasting remain the primary IGF-1 reduction strategies

AA.effect: f/f — shorter VDR protein, higher transcriptional activity
AA.interpretation: FokI 'f' allele produces shorter VDR protein initiating at Met4 instead of Met1 — functions more efficiently in transcription; associated with better immune response, vitamin D efficiency, and possibly lower cancer risk
recommendations: Lower supplemental vitamin D3 may be needed compared to FF genotype (higher transcriptional efficiency)
Target 40-60 ng/mL 25-OH-D rather than aggressive 70 ng/mL target

GA.interpretation: One shorter VDR variant — intermediate transcriptional activity
recommendations: Standard vitamin D3 2000-3000IU supplementation

GG.effect: F/F — longer, less efficient VDR protein
GG.interpretation: FokI 'F' allele produces longer VDR that initiates at Met1 — slightly less transcriptionally efficient; may require higher vitamin D levels for equivalent signaling
recommendations: Vitamin D3 3000-4000IU and target 50-70 ng/mL 25-OH-D
Magnesium 400mg co-supplementation for VDR activation

CC.interpretation: Common genotype for aging rate at CCDC26; baseline trajectory
recommendations: No action needed; standard aging rate management applies

CT.effect: One CCDC26 protective allele — modest aging rate benefit
CT.interpretation: One copy of the GWAS-identified protective allele for aging rate. Modest benefit observed in population studies (n=423,992, p=2e-55)
recommendations: One protective CCDC26 allele — generally favorable

TT.effect: Homozygous CCDC26 protective allele — maximum aging rate benefit
TT.interpretation: Homozygous for the GWAS protective allele at CCDC26. Strongest favorable association with aging rate
recommendations: Favorable CCDC26 variant — continue healthy lifestyle to maximise this genetic advantage

TT.interpretation: Most common genotype for aging rate at ZNG1A locus; no elevated GWAS risk signal
recommendations: No action needed — favorable common genotype

CT.effect: One ZNG1A risk allele — modest aging rate association
CT.interpretation: Carrier of one GWAS-identified risk allele for aging rate. Modest effect in population studies (n=423,992, p=1e-58)
recommendations: Awareness of modest aging rate risk variant; standard screening applies

CC.effect: Homozygous ZNG1A risk allele — elevated aging rate
CC.interpretation: Homozygous for the GWAS risk allele associated with aging rate. Strongest risk signal at the ZNG1A locus
recommendations: Discuss ZNG1A GWAS finding with a healthcare provider
Monitor aging rate via regular checkups

AA.interpretation: Common genotype for aging rate at CHCHD6; baseline trajectory
recommendations: No action needed; standard aging rate management applies

AG.effect: One CHCHD6 protective allele — modest aging rate benefit
AG.interpretation: One copy of the GWAS-identified protective allele for aging rate. Modest benefit observed in population studies (n=423,992, p=1e-51)
recommendations: One protective CHCHD6 allele — generally favorable

GG.effect: Homozygous CHCHD6 protective allele — maximum aging rate benefit
GG.interpretation: Homozygous for the GWAS protective allele at CHCHD6. Strongest favorable association with aging rate
recommendations: Favorable CHCHD6 variant — continue healthy lifestyle to maximise this genetic advantage

CC.interpretation: Common genotype for neuroimaging at MRC1; baseline trajectory
recommendations: No action needed; standard neuroimaging management applies

CT.effect: One MRC1 protective allele — strong neuroimaging benefit
CT.interpretation: One copy of the GWAS-identified protective allele for neuroimaging. Strong benefit observed in population studies (n=20,043, p=1e-55)
recommendations: One protective MRC1 allele — generally favorable

TT.effect: Homozygous MRC1 protective allele — maximum neuroimaging benefit
TT.interpretation: Homozygous for the GWAS protective allele at MRC1. Strongest favorable association with neuroimaging
recommendations: Favorable MRC1 variant — continue healthy lifestyle to maximise this genetic advantage

AA.interpretation: Common genotype for aging at FAM234A; baseline trajectory
recommendations: No action needed; standard aging management applies

AG.effect: One FAM234A protective allele — strong aging benefit
AG.interpretation: One copy of the GWAS-identified protective allele for aging. Strong benefit observed in population studies (n=25,460, p=3e-57)
recommendations: One protective FAM234A allele — generally favorable

GG.effect: Homozygous FAM234A protective allele — maximum aging benefit
GG.interpretation: Homozygous for the GWAS protective allele at FAM234A. Strongest favorable association with aging
recommendations: Favorable FAM234A variant — continue healthy lifestyle to maximise this genetic advantage

TT.interpretation: Most common genotype for aging at AXIN1 locus; no elevated GWAS risk signal
recommendations: No action needed — favorable common genotype

CT.effect: One AXIN1 risk allele — strong aging association
CT.interpretation: Carrier of one GWAS-identified risk allele for aging. Strong effect in population studies (n=25,460, p=1e-300)
recommendations: Awareness of modest aging risk variant; standard screening applies

CC.effect: Homozygous AXIN1 risk allele — elevated aging
CC.interpretation: Homozygous for the GWAS risk allele associated with aging. Strongest risk signal at the AXIN1 locus
recommendations: Discuss AXIN1 GWAS finding with a healthcare provider
Monitor aging via regular checkups

GG.interpretation: Common genotype for aging at HBG2; baseline trajectory
recommendations: No action needed; standard aging management applies

AG.effect: One HBG2 protective allele — strong aging benefit
AG.interpretation: One copy of the GWAS-identified protective allele for aging. Strong benefit observed in population studies (n=86,536, p=5e-51)
recommendations: One protective HBG2 allele — generally favorable

AA.effect: Homozygous HBG2 protective allele — maximum aging benefit
AA.interpretation: Homozygous for the GWAS protective allele at HBG2. Strongest favorable association with aging
recommendations: Favorable HBG2 variant — continue healthy lifestyle to maximise this genetic advantage
